quotes.verbatim_quote:
By continually writing out this material like this, you
gain a wider musical knowledge, and it helps your reading ability tremendously.
quotes.why_load_bearing: States Baker's transposition-by-writing rule and the rationale linking written practice to reading skill.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Work out each solo to itself. Then analyze it, take close notice as to how I connect my runs.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Directs the student to study run-to-run transitions, the load-bearing skill behind building longer solos.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
2 lot of places in those solos I am using riffs, while the chord changes for these measures are
completely foreign to the riff itself (See Lesson No. 38). But I have forced the riffs against the progressions.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Reinforces the chapter 11 “force against the chord” rule and tells the student where to spot it in the new material.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
study the riffs in each solo, then the chord progressions for those
measures. Then sing the riffs while you play the chord progressions. This way you can hear just how the riffs
sound against the chords.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Specifies the ear-training drill Baker treats as the verification step for any forced-riff usage.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
I am introducing a new position for solo work. This position is to be used for the keys of A4, Bé, C, Dé, and Ee
major ( it can be used for F, and G major but it is a little out of range).
quotes.why_load_bearing: Introduces an additional position with explicit key-applicability constraints, extending the position framework.

quotes.verbatim_quote: These riffs are in the key of Bé major and this position extends from the 3rd to the 6th frets on your Guitar.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Pins the fret-range of the newly introduced position, essential for locating the material on the fingerboard.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
When you go through this solo that I have written out, you will find that the first and the last courses are the
same. This is known as a groove riff. Groove riffs are repeated consecutively.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the chapter's new concept — the groove riff — and its structural placement at solo boundaries.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
You usually start and end a song with groove riffs. These kind of riffs are only used against Blues and vamp
changes (progression).
quotes.why_load_bearing: Gives the placement-and-applicability rule for groove riffs, scoping the technique to specific song forms.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
in the first and last 12 bars I am using the same run over and over again, That’s the way this
type of riff is used.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Quantifies the repetition pattern that operationalizes the groove riff in a blues form.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Now the tricky part about this solo is that it’s all done in the 3rd position on your Guitar (between the 3rd and
6th fret )
quotes.why_load_bearing: Constrains the execution position for the groove-riff solo, reinforcing the position-discipline rule.
